description: Chemistry grounding for the chemistry lens — bonding, reactions, equilibrium, thermodynamics, and the periodic structure behind them.

entries.title: The periodic table & periodic trends
entries.summary: Elements are ordered by atomic number; their chemistry is set by valence (outer) electrons, which repeat periodically. Down a group reactivity and atomic radius change predictably; across a period electronegativity and ionization energy rise. The table predicts how elements bond and react before any experiment — chemistry's organizing map.

entries.title: Chemical bonding: ionic, covalent, metallic
entries.summary: Atoms bond to reach stable electron configurations: ionic (electron transfer → charged ions held electrostatically, e.g. NaCl), covalent (electron sharing, polar if electronegativities differ), and metallic (a sea of delocalized electrons giving conductivity/malleability). Bond type predicts melting point, solubility, and conductivity.

entries.title: The mole & stoichiometry
entries.summary: A mole is 6.022×10²³ particles (Avogadro's number); it links the atomic scale to grams via molar mass. A balanced equation gives the fixed mole ratios of reactants and products, letting you compute yields and find the limiting reagent. Stoichiometry is the arithmetic backbone of every quantitative chemistry problem.

entries.title: Chemical equilibrium & Le Chatelier
entries.summary: Reversible reactions reach equilibrium where forward and reverse rates equal, characterized by K (products/reactants at equilibrium). Le Chatelier's principle: a system at equilibrium shifts to oppose an imposed change (add reactant → shift right; raise temperature → favor the endothermic direction). It governs industrial yield optimization (Haber process) and biological buffering.

entries.title: Acids, bases & pH
entries.summary: Brønsted acids donate protons, bases accept them; pH = −log[H⁺], with 7 neutral, lower acidic. Strong acids/bases dissociate fully; weak ones partially (characterized by Ka/Kb). A buffer (weak acid + conjugate base) resists pH change — the principle keeping blood near 7.4 and the basis of titration analysis.

entries.title: Thermodynamics: enthalpy, entropy & free energy
entries.summary: A reaction's spontaneity is set by Gibbs free energy ΔG = ΔH − TΔS: negative ΔG means spontaneous. Enthalpy ΔH is heat released/absorbed (bond energies); entropy ΔS is disorder. Temperature tips the balance — endothermic reactions can still run if they increase entropy enough. ΔG also sets the equilibrium constant (ΔG° = −RT ln K).

entries.title: Reaction kinetics & catalysis
entries.summary: Kinetics is how fast a reaction goes (vs thermodynamics' whether). Rate depends on concentration (rate law/order), temperature (Arrhenius: rate rises steeply with T), and activation energy — the barrier reactants must climb. A catalyst lowers that barrier without being consumed, speeding the reaction in both directions without shifting equilibrium.

entries.title: Oxidation-reduction (redox)
entries.summary: Redox reactions transfer electrons: oxidation is electron loss, reduction is gain (OIL RIG); they always pair. Separating the half-reactions across a circuit makes a battery (galvanic cell) or, run in reverse with input power, electrolysis. Redox underlies corrosion, combustion, respiration, and every battery and fuel cell.

entries.title: Organic chemistry: functional groups
entries.summary: Carbon's four bonds build chains, rings, and frameworks; reactivity localizes in functional groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, amine, etc.), so a molecule's chemistry is read from its groups, not its whole structure. Isomers (same formula, different arrangement) can behave entirely differently — the organizing logic behind biochemistry, pharmaceuticals, and polymers.

entries.title: States of matter & intermolecular forces
entries.summary: Whether a substance is solid, liquid, or gas at given conditions is set by the competition between thermal energy and intermolecular forces — London dispersion, dipole-dipole, and hydrogen bonding (strongest). Hydrogen bonding explains water's anomalously high boiling point, ice floating, and DNA base pairing — small force, outsized consequences.
